Name: curvature_aware_mixed_precision_quantization
Title: Curvature-Aware Adaptive Mixed Precision Quantization for Efficient Model Deployment
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curvature-aware approach to adaptive mixed precision quantization can significantly improve model performance and efficiency in a training-free manner, addressing the gap in understanding the relationship between quantization and model accuracy.
Related Work: This proposal builds upon recent advancements in training-free mixed precision quantization, including methods like InfoQ, which assess layer importance through mutual information, and EMQ, which focuses on proxy discovery. Our approach distinguishes itself by integrating curvature-aware optimization techniques to inform the quantization process, explicitly targeting the relationship between curvature properties and quantization impacts.
Abstract: In the quest for efficient neural network deployment, mixed precision quantization has emerged as a critical strategy. Traditional methods often rely on heuristic or training-based approaches that lack transparency and efficiency. This proposal introduces a curvature-aware adaptive mixed precision quantization framework, designed to optimize bit-width allocation based on curvature properties of the layer outputs. By utilizing a training-free methodology, we leverage spectral analysis to compute curvature metrics that guide the quantization process. This framework not only aims to reduce computation and memory overhead but also seeks to maintain or enhance model accuracy. We will validate our approach through extensive experimentation across various architectures, ensuring robust evaluation metrics that include accuracy retention, inference speed, and computational efficiency.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline mixed precision quantization framework and compare with the proposed curvature-aware method on standard datasets (e.g., ImageNet, CIFAR-10).
2. Conduct ablation studies varying the curvature metrics used to inform quantization decisions, assessing their impact on model performance.
3. Evaluate the proposed framework on different neural architectures (e.g., ResNet, MobileNet) to ensure generalizability.
4. Measure computational efficiency through FLOPs and memory usage, comparing against existing state-of-the-art methods.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the difficulty in accurately estimating curvature metrics for certain architectures, which may affect quantization decisions. Additionally, the proposed framework's performance is contingent on the quality of the curvature estimates, which may introduce variability. While the training-free nature aims to simplify deployment, the reliance on theoretical metrics may lead to suboptimal configurations in some edge cases.
